title: Chapter 28: The Golden Collapse
content: I should have been studying.

That's the thought that surfaces later, useless and small, when I try to reconstruct the night. I should have been studying for Dr. Adler's ethics final, the one I'd been dodging for three days because the irony of an ethics exam at Blackwood was too rich to sit with. Instead I was sprawled on my bed in dormitory C, reading the same paragraph of Hobbes for the fourth time, when the knock came.
Not Heath's knock. Heath knocked like he was picking a lock — two precise raps, pause, one more. This was different. Three knocks, uneven. Desperate.
I opened the door and found Julian Linton standing in the hallway with red-rimmed eyes and a face the color of old paper.

He wasn't supposed to be here. We hadn't spoken in eleven days — not since the night on the library landing when I told him it was over, when I watched something behind his ribs collapse and chose to keep walking. Eleven days of careful avoidance, of rerouting through the arts wing to skip the main hall, of eating dinner at odd hours. Eleven days of convincing myself that the ache in my chest was just withdrawal, not grief.
And now here he was, and he looked like he'd been dismantled from the inside.
"Can I come in?"

His voice was hoarse. Like he'd been talking for hours, or screaming, or both. I stepped aside without a word, and he walked past me into the small room, and the smell of him hit me — rain and sweat and something sharp underneath, like copper. Like he'd been running.

He didn't sit. He stood in the middle of my room with his hands in his jacket pockets and his shoulders curved inward, and I realized with a sick lurch that I was seeing Julian without his architecture. Every time I'd seen him before — in classrooms, at galas, in his father's study — he'd been constructed. Shoulders back, jaw set, smile calibrated. Now all of that was gone. What remained was something raw and trembling and furious.
"I asked my father a question," he said.
I closed the door. Leaned against it. My heart had started doing something unpleasant in my throat.

"I asked him why the Vance family is so important to us." He laughed, but it came out wrong — cracked, like a window taking a stone. "I thought I already knew the answer. Old families, shared history, the usual mythology. That's what I told myself. That's what I told *you*, when we were together. That it was just — social capital. Connections."
He pulled his hands from his pockets. His knuckles were white.

"So I asked him. Directly. And he told me the truth, Cat." Julian's eyes met mine, and the grief in them was so vast I nearly looked away. "He said: *Because we need their land. Blackwood sits on mineral rights worth nine figures. The school is a front.*"
The room went very quiet. I could hear the rain on the window, the distant hum of the heating pipes, my own pulse thick in my ears.

"He said it like it was obvious," Julian continued. "Like I'd asked him why we pay taxes. Like it was — administrative. Just business. The Vance family wasn't a partner or an ally or even a rival. We were a *holding company*. Blackwood was the asset. And the Lintons — my family — we've been buying board seats for forty years to keep that asset in play."

I didn't move. Didn't breathe. Because I'd known — some part of me had known since I first found the trust documents, since I traced the shell companies back to their donors and found Linton money at every node. But knowing it in the abstract was different from watching Julian say it aloud, watching the knowledge destroy him in real time.

"I went through the records after," he said. "Tax filings. Donation histories. Board meeting minutes — my father keeps copies in his study like trophies. The Linton family has funded Blackwood's board for decades. Every endowment, every capital campaign, every 'generous contribution to the future of education' — it was all an investment. Every scholarship was a PR cover. Every building with our name on it was a stake in the ground, a marker saying: *this land is ours when we're ready to collect.*"
His voice broke on the last word. He pressed the heel of his palm against his eye, hard, like he could push the tears back in.
It didn't work.

"My family's wealth isn't old money," he whispered. "It's extraction money. We mine. We take. We dig things out of the ground and leave holes. And I — I'm the heir. I'm the golden boy. I'm golden because someone else was *mined*."

There it was. The crack I'd been waiting for, the one I'd been trying to engineer from the start. The fracture line through Julian Linton's certainty. Only it wasn't satisfying. It wasn't a victory. It was just a boy standing in my room, weeping, and the sound of it made my teeth ache.
"Julian —"
"You were right." He said it like he was forcing the words through glass. "About all of it. The school, the board, my family — you were right. And I was too busy being in love with you to see it."
The word *love* landed between us like something dropped from a great height. Heavy. Final. Irretrievable.

He wasn't looking at me. He was looking at his hands, at the fine gold watch on his wrist — the one his father gave him for his sixteenth birthday, the one I'd always thought was just another marker of Linton excess. Now I could see it for what it was: a collar. Beautiful and gilded and tight.

"I spent three years defending them to you," he said. "Three years explaining away every red flag, every weird board decision, every time the school's interests lined up with Linton Industries'. I told you it was coincidence. I told you my family was complicated but fundamentally good. I told myself that." He swallowed. "And the whole time, they were just — waiting. Keeping the land warm until they could crack it open."

I stayed against the door. I didn't go to him. Part of me wanted to — the part that remembered his hands, his laugh, the way he'd look at me like I was the only solid thing in a world made of smoke. But the part that had spent months building the case against Blackwood, against his family, against everything he represented — that part understood that going to him now would be mercy, and mercy wasn't what this moment needed.
What this moment needed was the truth, undiluted.
"I knew some of it," I said quietly.
His head came up. Not angry. Not even surprised. Just — hollow.

"I knew the Lintons were connected to the board. I knew the donations weren't charity. I didn't have the mineral rights — that's new. But I knew enough. And I didn't tell you because I thought you'd choose them over me. I thought if I made you pick, you'd pick the gold."
Something moved across his face. Not rage. Not accusation. Just recognition, terrible and complete.
"You did," he said. Not a question.
"Yes."

"And I would have. Then." He wiped his face with his sleeve — a graceless, un-Julian gesture that made my chest tighten. "You were right not to trust me. I wouldn't have believed you. I would've told my father, and he would've —" He stopped. The silence that followed was worse than anything he could have said, because I could see him finishing the sentence in his head, and the answer was: *he would have made it worse. He would have protected the investment.*
"Julian," I said again, and this time I didn't stop myself. I crossed the room and stood in front of him, close enough to see the salt tracks on his cheeks, the slight tremor in his jaw. "I'm sorry."
"Don't." The word came out raw. "Don't apologize for being right."
"I'm not apologizing for being right. I'm apologizing for how I did it. For making you collateral damage before you could be an ally. For treating you like a target instead of a person."
The words hung in the air between us, and I realized they were the most honest thing I'd said to him in three years. Maybe ever.

He looked at me. Really looked — not through the filter of want or wounded pride or the thousand small performances we'd built between us. Just looked, the way you look at someone when you've got nothing left to protect.
"I want to help," he said.
I blinked. "What?"
"Whatever you're doing. Whatever you and Heath are planning — I want in."

Heath's name in Julian's mouth was strange, like hearing a foreign word spoken by someone who'd only just learned it. But Julian didn't flinch. His jaw was set. His tears were still wet on his face, but his eyes had changed — the grief was still there, but under it, something harder. Something that burned.
"Not because I love you," he said, and the past tense landed like a door closing. "Not anymore. Because I want to tear down the thing that made my family what it is."
I stared at him. The golden boy of Blackwood Academy, heir to Linton Industries, standing in my room with his face still wet and his voice still broken, asking to burn his own house down.

I should have said no. Every strategic instinct I had was screaming it — Julian was compromised, volatile, his motives tangled beyond usefulness. He was a liability. Heath would say no. My father would say no. The logical answer was no.

But I looked at him — at the tremor in his hands, at the way he carried his grief like something heavier than his body could bear — and I thought: *he's not asking to help me. He's asking to survive.*
"I'll ask him," I said. "It's not just my call."
Julian held my gaze for a long moment. Then something that might have been a smile — exhausted, wrecked, barely there — crossed his face.
"That's the first honest thing you've said to me in three years."

The words hit me in the sternum. I didn't deny it. I couldn't. He was right. Three years of half-truths and strategic omissions, three years of loving him in the spaces between lies. Three years of building a war and never telling him it was coming.
Julian straightened. Pulled his jacket tighter, like armor made of cloth. He wasn't any less broken — I could see that clearly. But he was standing, and sometimes standing is all you can do.
"I'll wait," he said. "However long. I'll wait."
Then he walked past me, out the door, into the hallway. I heard his footsteps — uneven, slower than his usual clipped stride — recede toward the stairwell.
I stood in the doorway.

The hallway was empty and cold, the fluorescent lights buzzing above in that particular pitch that always made my teeth itch. Through the window at the end of the corridor, I could see Julian emerge from the building's side door into the rain. He didn't run. Didn't hurry. Just walked, head up, into the dark.
Golden boy. Shattered. Still standing.

I watched until he disappeared around the corner of the arts wing, until the rain swallowed the last trace of him, until there was nothing left but wet pavement and the sour taste of copper at the back of my throat.
*I broke him,* I thought. *But he's choosing to stay broken in a way that means something.*
I closed the door. Picked up my phone from the bed. My hands weren't shaking, but they wanted to.
**Julian knows. He wants to help. Thoughts?**
I hit send before I could second-guess myself. My thumb left a faint smear of condensation on the screen — rain, from where I'd pressed it against the window.
The reply came in eleven seconds. Heath was awake. Of course he was.
**Trust him?**
I read the word three times. Trust. Such a small word for such an impossible calculation. Trust Julian to do what? To fight? To stay? To not run back to his father the first time the cost got real?

I thought about the look on his face when he said *extraction money*. I thought about the way he'd said *not anymore* — not with regret but with the kind of finality that leaves a scar. I thought about grief that genuine, rage that clean, and I understood something I hadn't before:
Julian wasn't coming to us because he'd lost me. He was coming because he'd lost himself, and the only way back was through the fire.
I typed my reply slowly, each letter deliberate.
**I trust that he hates them more than he loves me. That's enough.**

I set the phone down. Stared at the ceiling. The rain kept coming, drumming against the glass in arrhythmic bursts, and somewhere in the dark Julian Linton was walking through it — not toward me, not away from me, but toward something that would unmake everything he'd ever been.
A true believer with nothing to lose.
I pulled the Hobbes back into my lap, but the words swam. All I could think was: *weapons are unpredictable. And the most dangerous weapon is the one that chooses to fire.*
